items.title: What Began as a 'Tree Orphanage' Has Grown Into a Forest of 2,000 Trees & 500+ Guardians
items.blurb: In Santiniketan, where the red earth glows beneath century-old shade, and Rabindranath Tagore's vision of learning under trees still lingers, 29-year-old Somnath Ghosh is nurturing something both practical and poetic — a home for trees that people plant and then forget. His Brikkho Foundation calls itself India's first "Orphanage Home for Trees." The phrase sounds metaphorical until Ghosh explains what it means. A tree, he says, becomes orphaned in two ways: when it is cut without thought, and when it is planted ceremonially, only to be abandoned once the photographs are taken. For Ghosh, every tree deserves more than a plantation drive. It deserves a guardian. "If society has homes for orphaned children," he asks, "why can it not have a system of guardianship for neglected trees?" Brikkho Foundation calls itself India's first "Orphanage Home for Trees." For him, Brikkho is that family — one that plants, nurtures, monitors and protects. The goal is not simply to increase the number of trees, but to build lasting relationships between people and nature. A dream that grew from the forest That relationship did not begin with Brikkho. It began years earlier, with a difficult choice. Ghosh had painstakingly built a multimedia studio, assembling cameras, drones and editing systems piece by piece. It represented years of hard work and financial struggle. But another calling kept growing louder. "If I do not act right now, there is absolutely no guarantee I will be able to do it in the future," he recalls. His love for plants and animals had taken root in childhood. Later, while working on an assignment with the Burdwan Forest Department, that quiet affection became something far more urgent. Living inside the forest changed him. He also noticed something else. Many people genuinely wanted to plant trees, but very few knew how to ensure those saplings survived. The enthusiasm was there. The support system was not. That gap would eventually become Brikkho Foundation. A turning point during the pandemic Like many life-changing journeys, his gathered momentum during the COVID-19 lockdown. In Santiniketan, Ghosh and a group of volunteers formed Helping Hands. Many people genuinely wanted to plant trees, but very few knew how to ensure those saplings survived. The enthusiasm was there. The support system was not. For 150 consecutive days, they fed stray dogs left hungry after tea stalls and eateries shut down. They delivered medicines to elderly residents, spread awareness about masks, and later carried relief materials to the Sundarbans after Cyclone Amphan. The work extended far beyond environmental conservation. "The emotional and spiritual nourishment I received during that period reshaped who I am," he says. "It amplified my desire to dedicate myself completely to this cause." That experience strengthened his belief that caring for nature could never be separated from caring for people. Planting is only the beginning When Brikkho Foundation was formally established, Ghosh wanted to solve a problem that had long bothered him. Too often, tree plantation ended the moment a sapling entered the ground. For him, that was where the real work should begin. Today, Brikkho's adoption model brings together the digital and physical worlds. People can adopt a native tree through Brikkho Foundation for a one-time contribution of Rs 600. People can adopt native tree species online, while the Foundation plants them in clusters, currently focusing on the Sonajhuri forest — a landscape named after the Acacia auriculiformis , where golden blossoms fall like drops of gold. The trees are planted on land provided by the Forest Department as well as on land voluntarily made available by local farmers for afforestation. When Brikkho Foundation was formally established, Ghosh wanted to solve a problem that had long bothered him. The Foundation continues caring for each sapling through regular audits, soil preparation before the monsoon, fencing repairs, and organic inputs until the trees can thrive independently. Each adopted tree is monitored and maintained for three years, ensuring its survival and healthy growth until it becomes self-sustaining. So far, it has planted more than 2,000 trees, maintaining a 95 percent survival rate. Brikkho Foundation has been actively working since 2021, and these nearly 2,000 trees have been planted between 2021 and 2026. Around 500–600 individuals have adopted trees through the programme. As the plantations are spread across multiple locations, the Foundation does not attribute them to a single acreage. Many adopters plant trees in memory of loved ones and return years later to watch them grow. "I adopted a Mahogany tree on my birthday in January 2022 by paying a one-time fee. Four years have passed since then, and the tree has now become an adult. I received a certificate for the adoption, which I’ve framed on the wall of my home in Kolkata, and I still get regular updates from the Brikkho Foundation about its progress," says Prosun Chanda, 27, who works for a Kolkata-based media outlet. To strengthen accountability, Brikkho has also developed a "Track My Plant" feature that allows adopters to follow each tree through geo-tagged photographs and regular updates. In addition, every adopter receives a Green Hero Certificate, the geo-tagged location of the adopted tree, online tracking through Brikkho's website, and periodic plantation updates. Ghosh says the feature addresses one of the biggest shortcomings of large plantation drives. Too often, nobody knows what happens after the planting ceremony ends. Today, many of Brikkho's adopters come from metropolitan cities, the Bengali diaspora, and Indian communities living overseas. Building forests, not plantations For Ghosh, success is measured not by the number of trees planted but by the kind of forest they become. Brikkho deliberately avoids monoculture plantations. Instead, it creates layered ecosystems using Neem, Shal, Kadam, Banyan, Peepal and native shrubs that support birds, insects and microorganisms. Resilience, he believes, lies in diversity. Many adopters plant trees in memory of loved ones and return years later to watch them grow. "A forest of 10,000 trees is beautiful," he says, "but it becomes revolutionary when it is built by 10,000 conscious individuals who each own a piece of its heartbeat." For him, the smallest unit of change is not an acre of land. It is one person connected to one living tree. The Foundation's forest land lies in the Kankalitala area of Bolpur, where adopted trees continue to take root and grow. Its work has also received formal recognition and approval from the Forest Department of the Government of India. From forests to rivers As the Foundation grew, so did its mission. Through Mission Clean Santiniketan, attention shifted to the Kopai River viewpoint — a distributary of the Ganges celebrated in Tagore's writings but increasingly choked by plastic waste. For an entire year, volunteers cleaned the stretch every weekend. The Kopai River Clean-up Campaign was carried out continuously for approximately seven months, during which volunteers collected waste in large garbage bags over nearly 120–150 clean-up days. Today, in collaboration with the Wildlife Institute of India, the initiative is working to restore the river through better waste management, including dustbins, collection systems and recycling. Beyond these efforts, Brikkho Foundation has conducted environmental awareness programmes across 10 villages and carried out plantation activities in five to six villages around Santiniketan. Similar clean-up drives have also been carried out in the Sonajhuri forest and around the Kanch Mandir. On humid afternoons, volunteers bend to collect plastic wrappers left behind by tourists, filling sacks with discarded bottles and fishing lines that clog the river. The work doesn't stop there. They also visit tribal hamlets on the forest's edge, listening to elders speak about seed preservation and traditional farming practices. In return, they share practical knowledge on organic composting and crop rotation, creating an exchange where indigenous wisdom and modern methods complement each other. Beyond these efforts, Brikkho Foundation has conducted environmental awareness programmes across 10 villages and carried out plantation activities in five to six villages around Santiniketan. Caring for people alongside nature That same philosophy shapes the Foundation's community work. Santiniketan's cultural landscape is woven together by Bengali traditions and tribal heritage. Through the Share Happiness Programme, food is distributed across villages in Birbhum district, including Beldanga, Baduriya, Dunaipur, Saheb Danga and Amradanga. Some villages are predominantly Adivasi, while others are more diverse, but together they reflect the communities that surround Santiniketan. Since the programme began, Brikkho Foundation has distributed more than 5,000 food packets to beneficiaries across these communities. For volunteers, however, the most memorable moments are often the simplest. Children waiting eagerly for food packets. Elders sharing folk songs while receiving medicines. The initiative reflects Ghosh's belief that ecological restoration and social care cannot exist separately. Looking towards 2050 The sight of Santiniketan's century-old trees slowly disappearing due to age, storms and neglect inspired another long-term vision. Mission Green Santiniketan 2050 asks a simple question: If today's giants disappear, what will replace them? Its answer is equally simple. Plant now so that twenty years from today, another canopy is already waiting. The mission hopes to create not only greener landscapes but also a cooler microclimate, healthier public spaces and continuity with Tagore's vision of Santiniketan. Like many grassroots movements, Brikkho's greatest challenge remains funding. Like many grassroots movements, Brikkho's greatest challenge remains funding. Saplings themselves are affordable. Protecting them is not. Fencing, maintenance, labour and long-term monitoring all require sustained financial support. Ghosh began the Foundation largely through self-funding. Even today, the initiative is primarily self-funded by Somnath Ghosh, with additional support coming through tree adoption contributions, individual donations and donations received specifically for food distribution drives. The Rs 600 adoption contribution covers the complete cost of planting, protecting, monitoring and maintaining a tree for three years, by which time it generally becomes self-sustaining. The Foundation's biggest recurring expenses include tree maintenance and monitoring, fencing and protection, sapling preparation and plantation, website maintenance, office operations, and travel and transportation for plantation monitoring and the movement of saplings. As it grows, crowdfunding and public support have become increasingly important. Yet he continues to draw strength from Tagore's Ekla Cholo Re — walk alone if you must. Today, around 50 volunteers — students, plantation experts, retired officers and schoolteachers — stand beside him. As Arup Kumar Thandar, District Coordinator, West Bengal Biodiversity Board, says: "What is remarkable about the Foundation is that its volunteers go far beyond planting or adopting trees. They take on the less glamorous tasks too — clearing forest floors, cleaning rivers and walking into tribal hamlets to learn from traditional wisdom." When people tell Ghosh that one person, or even one tree, cannot make much difference, he smiles. The ocean, he says, is formed drop by drop. "I dream of a future when Santiniketan's sons and daughters—and admirers of Tagore's vision and poetry across the world—will join hands to make this abode of learning greener. Their contributions will not only sustain our movement but also renew the timeless bond between knowledge and nature."

items.title: Rescued From the Floods, With Nowhere To Sleep: Assam's 7.2 Lakh Survivors Urgently Need Shelter Now
items.blurb: At around 7 pm on 21 July, floodwaters entered Rohini Duwara’s home in Lakwa Abhayapuri village in Assam’s Charaideo district. His family tried to move towards higher ground as the powerful current rose around them. Rohini’s wife, Tutumoni Duwara, and their daughter, Bidisha, were swept away first. His sons, Bidyut and Biplab, entered the water in an attempt to save them, but the current carried them away too. In a matter of minutes, Rohini was separated from his wife and three children. The bodies of Bidisha, Bidyut and Biplab were later recovered, while the search for Tutumoni continued at the time of the latest available report. Across Assam, thousands of families are living through their own hours of fear. Homes have disappeared under floodwater, livelihoods have been washed away, and people have waited for rescue on rooftops, embankments and patches of higher ground. The floods have left behind staggering numbers. Behind each one is a family wondering where it will sleep, how it will protect its children and what will remain when the water recedes. Across several villages, people have abandoned their homes after water entered every room. Photograph: (Oxomiya Jiyori) For many, the first step towards surviving the coming days begins with finding a roof. The Better India has joined hands with DonateKart to support the Assam-based Association of Social and Human Activists, or ASHA, in procuring emergency tents for displaced families. Your contribution can give a family a dry place to sleep, protect its food and medicines, and stay together while floodwaters continue to surround their home. Tap to Donate When rivers spread through villages, farms and homes Weeks of heavy rain across Assam and the hills upstream have pushed the Brahmaputra and its tributaries far beyond their usual paths. The Dikhow, Disang and Janji have spilt into villages, tea estates and farmland across Upper Assam. In many places, residents say they have never seen water spread this far. Satellite images show the scale of the flooding. Near Jorhat, water from the Brahmaputra stretched across nearly 21 kilometres, about three times its earlier visible spread. That expanse includes flooded fields, sandbars and nearby settlements, showing how far the river has entered people’s everyday lives. By 24 July, more than 7.21 lakh people across 11 districts and nearly 900 villages had been affected, according to Assam’s Disaster Reporting and Information Management System. At least 47 people had died, while several others remained missing. More than 25,000 hectares of farmland lay underwater, over 24,000 people were living in relief camps, and nearly 1.39 lakh animals had been affected. Assam Drowns in Deadly Floods. 41 dead, over 7 lakh people affected as floods swallow 11 districts. Rivers still rising. Assam is hurting. Homes are gone, lives have been changed forever and even innocent animals have been lost. Please keep every affected family in your… pic.twitter.com/yAt3PCkyvb — زماں (@Delhiite_) July 24, 2026 Sivasagar was the worst-affected district, with approximately 3.92 lakh people impacted. Charaideo followed with 1.10 lakh people affected, while nearly 97,700 people were impacted in Jorhat. The exceptional rainfall across Upper Assam has added enormous volumes of water to rivers already struggling to contain it. India Meteorological Department data showed that Charaideo received around 170 mm of rain during the period analysed, which was 436% above normal. Golaghat recorded 147.9 mm, Jorhat 105.2 mm and Sivasagar 93.2 mm. For the families living through the flood, these figures describe only a fraction of what has been lost. ‘People need shelter and drinking water too’ Rescue teams are still working across flood-hit districts to reach families cut off by the water. The National Disaster Response Force, State Disaster Response Force, Army, Border Security Force, local police and other agencies have been evacuating stranded people. Helicopters are also carrying food packets to villages that can no longer be reached by road. ASHA is supporting these relief efforts on the ground in partnership with DonateKart. Rajib Kalita, Managing Director of ASHA, tells The Better India that calls for help continue to come in from communities across the state. “People are calling the SDRF and NDRF continuously, asking to be rescued. Every agency is trying its best, but the sheer volume of water has made rescue operations extremely difficult,” he says. Even when food reaches families, another problem begins. They often have nowhere dry to keep it. Weeks of heavy rain across Assam and the hills upstream have pushed the Brahmaputra and its tributaries far beyond their usual paths. Photograph: (PTI) “Food packets are dropped from helicopters, but many become wet after falling into floodwaters. Even when food reaches families, they do not have a dry place to keep it or eat it,” Kalita explains. Relief workers are now hearing the same request again and again. “People keep telling us, ‘Do not think only about food. What we need right now is drinking water and shelter too,’” he shares. Families need a protected place where they can eat, sleep and keep food, medicines and clothes dry. While rescue work continues, a temporary tent can give them that immediate sense of safety. Tap to Donate How your contribution will help Relief workers across flood-hit Assam say shelter has become one of the most urgent needs. To help families find a safe place to stay, The Better India is working with DonateKart to support ASHA’s relief efforts. With your help, we aim to raise Rs 2 lakh to procure emergency tents for families who have been forced to leave their homes behind. ASHA and its partner organisations will distribute these shelters across affected villages, reaching families who are still waiting for a dry and secure place to rest. Many escaped with only a few belongings. Some remain stranded on tin rooftops or small patches of higher ground, watching the water close in around their homes. The floods have also severely affected livestock, with 1,38,965 animals impacted and 540 animals, including 500 poultry birds, reported washed away. Photograph: (PTI) Food may reach them through rescue teams, but without shelter, even that becomes difficult to manage. Families have nowhere dry to store supplies, prepare a meal or put their children to sleep safely. Every contribution will help procure a temporary shelter that can give a family: A dry place where children and elderly people can sleep Protection from rain, mosquitoes and contaminated floodwater A secure space to store drinking water, food, clothes and medicines Temporary accommodation while the family waits for the water to recede or prepares to rebuild Your donation will directly support the procurement of emergency tents for flood-affected families in Assam. Tap to Donate When a tent becomes the safest place a family has Floodwater enters a home and changes every part of daily life. Beds become unusable. Clothes, documents and medicines are soaked or lost. Food cannot be kept dry. The rooms where families once cooked, ate and slept together become impossible to live in. Across several villages, people have left their homes after water entered every room. In some places, even pucca (permanent) houses have offered little protection, leaving families stranded on rooftops as they wait for rescue boats. “Even people with pucca houses are sitting on their roofs because everything below is underwater,” says Kalita. For families without shelter, the risks continue even after they reach higher ground. They remain exposed to rain, mosquitoes, contaminated water and illness. Children have nowhere dry to sleep. People who need regular medication have no safe place to store it. Families struggle to protect food packets, clothes and the few belongings they managed to carry. This is why relief workers say tents are now among the most urgent needs. “If families have a tent, they at least have a place where children can sleep, where food can remain dry and where they can live with some dignity until the water recedes,” Kalita explains. For a family waiting to understand whether its home can be entered, repaired or rebuilt, a tent can offer safety, privacy and a dry place to stay through the days ahead. Tap to Donate Rescue is only the beginning Floodwaters may begin to recede, but many families will remain displaced for weeks. In Assam’s low-lying areas, water often stays long after the first surge. Homes may be unsafe to return to, fields may remain buried under mud and drinking-water sources may become contaminated. The India Meteorological Department has also forecast more rain, thunderstorms and lightning across Assam through 29 July. With the ground already saturated, another spell of rain could push water levels up again. Very heartbreaking scene from #AssamFloods , almost 500 cows swept away from a single village in Sivsagar district. The song lyrics in the background of late Zubeen da aptly describes the scene 👇 “By the string of fate... the floods have returned, Sweeping away the entire… pic.twitter.com/1Z4AUrTEI5 — Oxomiya Jiyori 🇮🇳 (@SouleFacts) July 24, 2026 For families who have already fled once, that may mean packing up whatever little they have and moving all over again. Kalita says these are often the weeks when public attention starts to fade, even though families still need shelter, clean water and basic supplies. “The water will not disappear tomorrow. Even after 10 days, many families will still be displaced. By then, media attention may reduce, but their problems will not,” he says. A rescue boat may carry a family out of the flood, but many still end up living beside roads, on embankments or in crowded relief camps, unsure of when they can return home. During these uncertain days, a temporary shelter can give them a dry and protected place to stay. A safe roof for the days ahead For Rohini Duwara, the evening of 21 July changed everything. His family’s story puts a human face to Assam’s flood maps and rainfall figures. It shows how a familiar evening at home can turn, within minutes, into a loss that shapes every day that follows. Across the state, thousands of other families escaped with only the clothes they were wearing. Some are still waiting for rescue boats. Others have reached higher ground, but have nowhere safe or dry to stay. “The government has mobilised every possible agency, and many people are trying to help,” Kalita says. “But this is a disaster of enormous scale. Recovery will take time.” Until families can return home, an emergency tent can give them the protection they urgently need. It can give a child a dry place to sleep, help parents keep food and medicines safe, and allow families to stay together as they face the days ahead. Your contribution can help give a displaced family a safe roof and a place to begin recovering while the waters recede. items.title: When a Highway Threatened Dehradun’s 100-Yo Sal Trees, Residents Brought Back the ‘Chipko’ Spirit
items.blurb: Saat Mod, or "seven bends", is a short, shaded stretch of National Highway 7 between Bhaniyawala and Rishikesh in Dehradun district. For years it was little more than a bend in the road to most travellers, a place where Sal trees lean over the tarmac, and elephants occasionally amble across between forest patches. In July 2026, this quiet stretch became the site of one of Uttarakhand's most remarkable displays of citizen action in recent memory. The Bhaniyawala-Jolly Grant-Rishikesh stretch was being widened from two lanes to four or six, a project meant to ease growing traffic to Jolly Grant Airport and along the Char Dham Yatra route. But the widening meant thousands of mature Sal trees, some over a century old, standing in its path. The stretch also falls within a recognised elephant corridor connecting to the Rajaji Tiger Reserve, a link that allows wildlife to move safely across the wider landscape. Rather than watch the trees disappear, residents of Dehradun decided to show up. Every single day. A movement rooted in memory What unfolded at Saat Mod was not a single dramatic gesture but a sustained, creative campaign that drew on decades of local tradition. Students, retired officials, environmentalists and everyday residents began gathering at the site, and their methods were as striking as their persistence. Many protesters hugged the trees earmarked for felling, a direct echo of the Chipko movement of the 1970s, when villagers in these same Himalayan foothills wrapped their arms around trunks to physically stop loggers. The protest drew students, retired government officials and environmentalists together, a cross-generational mix rarely seen in single-issue roadside demonstrations. That movement began in Uttarakhand and became one of India's most iconic examples of grassroots environmentalism . Its return to the same hills, five decades later, was not lost on anyone at Saat Mod. Alongside the tree-hugging, protesters revived another local custom: tying sacred threads around the trunks of trees, a gesture traditionally used to mark a tree as protected, almost like a pledge of guardianship. Each thread became a small, personal commitment, a way of saying this tree matters, long before any formal outcome was decided. The most inventive moment came on 16 July, the date of Harela, Uttarakhand's annual festival celebrating greenery, agriculture and the arrival of the monsoon. Instead of the usual festivities, protesters observed what they called Kala Harela , or "black Harela", dressing entirely in black to mark their mourning for trees being felled even as the festival meant to honour nature was being celebrated elsewhere. Together, these acts turned a roadside protest into something closer to a cultural performance, one that used memory, ritual and symbolism as effectively as any placard or petition. What the numbers show Environmental researcher Anoop Nautiyal shared data pointing out that Uttarakhand had diverted more than 46,000 hectares of forest land for development since 2000, with nearly half of that concentrated in Dehradun district alone. The protesters' core demand was not to stop the highway outright but to ensure gentler alternatives had genuinely been explored first. For many at Saat Mod, this context was part of why they felt compelled to act , not simply about one stretch of highway, but about what collective vigilance could still achieve for the hills they called home. Plans for the highway itself included measures designed with conservation in mind: a proposed 3.5-kilometre elephant underpass, wildlife crossings, culverts and compensatory afforestation across 40 hectares, developed with input from WWF-India and the Wildlife Institute of India. These details mattered to protesters too. Their questions were less about opposing progress and more about ensuring every gentler alternative had genuinely been explored first. What citizens achieved by simply not leaving After days of sustained, peaceful presence at Saat Mod, tree felling was paused and fresh consultations were opened with residents, experts and other stakeholders. For a protest built almost entirely on showing up, standing still and tying a thread, that pause was a meaningful marker. The story of Saat Mod is not yet finished, and the trees already felled cannot simply grow back. But what happened here offers something worth holding onto: a reminder that ordinary people, using nothing more than presence, memory and a sense of shared purpose, can still shape decisions that once felt entirely out of their hands. The Chipko movement began in these same hills fifty years ago and went on to influence forest policy across the country. At Saat Mod, a new generation picked up the same instinct, tying it to old rituals and new symbolism, and proved that collective, peaceful action still has the power to make people pause and listen. items.title: Gen Z Volunteers Clean Up Jantar Mantar, Showing Civic Sense Starts With Action
items.blurb: The protests, led by thousands of NEET UG aspirants and students seeking greater transparency and accountability in medical entrance examinations, have drawn large crowds to Delhi’s Jantar Mantar over the past few days. Young people from across the country have travelled to the capital, gathering with placards, water bottles and backpacks in hand, united by a common cause. Large public gatherings mostly leave behind another, less talked-about reality. Once the crowds disperse, roads and pavements are usually littered with plastic bottles, paper cups, food wrappers and discarded banners, leaving sanitation workers to clean up the aftermath. But this time, a different story received attention. The morning after the gathering, a group of Gen Z protesters returned to Jantar Mantar carrying brooms, garbage bags and gloves. Instead of walking away from the litter, they swept the roads, collected waste and cleaned the public space they had used just hours earlier. According to Mint , videos of the clean-up quickly went viral, with many people praising the youngsters for taking responsibility instead of expecting someone else to do it. The volunteers were seen filling garbage bags with plastic bottles, paper waste and food packaging, working together until the area looked visibly cleaner. For many, those few minutes of sweeping became one of the most memorable images from the gathering. When civic sense takes centre stage In India, conversations around cleanliness usually begin with government campaigns or municipal workers. But the responsibility for keeping public spaces clean belongs to everyone who uses them. The morning after the gathering, a group of Gen Z protesters returned to Jantar Mantar carrying brooms, garbage bags and gloves. Photograph: (Image generated by AI) The young volunteers at Jantar Mantar seemed to understand exactly that. There was no formal cleanliness drive or official instruction asking participants to return. Those who came back did so voluntarily, picking up brooms and garbage bags because they believed the space should not be left littered after such a large gathering. It was a small act, but one that carried a strong message. Speaking up for a cause and caring for a shared public space need not be separate acts. They can go hand in hand. Gen Z is changing what responsibility looks like Gen Z is described as outspoken, digitally connected and unafraid to question the status quo. But increasingly, the generation is also showing that responsibility extends beyond raising awareness. Whether it is organising neighbourhood clean-ups, volunteering during natural disasters, participating in climate campaigns or promoting sustainable living, many young Indians are pairing advocacy with action. The scenes from the protest site reflected that mindset. Instead of leaving a mess for someone else to clean up, the volunteers chose to clean up after themselves . The act required no elaborate planning or resources. Just a few brooms, garbage bags and people willing to spend their time making a public space cleaner. That decision resonated with thousands online. The little things that often go unnoticed The clean-up was not the only act of kindness witnessed around Jantar Mantar. According to The Times of India , volunteers spent the day helping one another cope with Delhi’s intense heat. Bamboo hand fans were distributed among participants, drinking water and food were shared freely, and residents opened their homes so women could use washrooms when needed. There was no formal cleanliness drive or official instruction asking participants to return. Photograph: (Image generated by AI) These gestures rarely become the biggest headlines, but together they revealed another side of the gathering. One built on cooperation, empathy and looking out for strangers. Cleaning the venue the following morning became a natural extension of that spirit. Leaving a place better than you found it Environmental movements across the world promote a simple principle. Leave no trace. Whether it is trekkers carrying their waste down a mountain, volunteers cleaning beaches after weekends, or neighbourhood groups restoring public parks, the idea remains the same. Every shared space deserves care from the people who use it. The Gen Z volunteers embodied that principle in the heart of the capital. Their actions act as a reminder that civic sense is not measured by grand gestures. It is reflected in everyday choices. Picking up a discarded bottle instead of stepping over it. Sweeping a pavement instead of assuming someone else will. Leaving behind not litter, but an example. Cleaning the venue the following morning became a natural extension of that spirit. Photograph: (Image generated by AI) Long after photographs of crowds fade from memory, it may well be these images that remain. Young people with brooms in their hands, peacefully cleaning a public space, proving that responsibility does not end when the gathering is over. Sometimes, the most touching statement is not made through words. Sometimes, it is made by leaving a place cleaner than you found it.

items.title: Meet the 102-YO Lawyer Who Ran a Secret Jungle Radio During Goa’s Freedom Struggle
items.blurb: On 19 December 1961, the people of Goa looked towards the sky, baffled at the downpour. Leaflets were raining down. Each bore a message: ‘Rejoice! Rejoice! Brothers and sisters. Goa is free after 450 years.’ The news rained down from a plane carrying two messengers of freedom: Libia Lobo Sardesai and Vaman Sardesai, who would later marry each other. Both played instrumental roles in Goa’s liberation movement, which sought to free the state from Portuguese rule after India gained independence in 1947. I observe Libia while she retells this riveting tale; it’s almost as if it were just yesterday that she’d flown over Goa in that plane. Neither time nor age has tarnished that memory in the slightest. I must admit, securing this interview was akin to finding the treasure at the end of a 20-month-long hunt. It began the day I stumbled upon a feature about the luminary in Vogue. I then began scouting for anyone who could put me in touch with her, eventually chancing upon someone who knew someone who knew one of Libia’s grandnieces. She put me in touch with the legend herself. Before I knew it, I was taking a trip to Goa to meet Libia. It’s not every day that you meet a centenarian. It’s definitely not every day that you meet one who has been integral to shaping a state’s identity. Libia Lobo Sardesai’s story Condensing decades' worth of history into a one-hour story is no mean feat. And I half expect Libia’s recounting to be sporadic, but no. She’s like a history book, with the chronology intact, and each one of her stories makes for a great page-turner. The only details she grows hazy about are the accolades. As she tells me, the awards and recognition have continued to pour in over the years, but she has never been one to keep count. But even when she does forget to mention a win or two, the walls and shelves of her home oblige. I gaze around and take in pictures of Vaman and Libia being awarded the Padma Shri, which Vaman was awarded in 1992 and Libia in 2025. There are also gallantry medals by the Indian Army and Navy, certificates, invitations to address events, and several other awards. But the biggest recognition of all sits right across from the home, emblazoned on the wall of a building — a giant mural by street artist Solomon Souza. It’s a younger version of the 102-year-old standing in front of me. Though the two look strikingly different, they share the same unmistakable sprightliness. And as we stand on the balcony, hugged by the Goa breeze, Libia begins her tale. For years, Libia Lobo Sardesai ran an underground radio station, spreading uncensored news and helping fuel Goa’s fight for liberation. “I was schooling in Bombay (now Mumbai) when the freedom struggle was on. Even though I was just 12 years old at the time, I watched patriots die for their country. The visuals are still clear in my mind: hordes of people dressed in white and khaki , walking alongside Gandhi ji from Chowpatty to Dhobi Talao, shouting slogans of ‘Jai Hind’ (Long Live India) and ‘Inquilab Zindabad’ (Long Live the Revolution). We’d watch as the police commissioners would come on horseback and trample upon them. They would fall and get wounded and die. These visuals stuck in my mind; they were like seeds that grew as I grew.” Her voice betrays a sadness as she continues, “I did not know exactly what the movement was all about. I did not understand what terms like colonialism meant. But what my friends and I did understand was that we were being ruled by a foreign power, and we wanted freedom.” The Bombay girl with a Goan heart Right before I made my way to Libia’s home, I had been spending time with my editor’s toddler. She was amused when I told her I’d be interviewing a 102-year-old later that day. “That’s so many years,” she remarked. I silently shared in her amusement. “It’s almost like you’ve been a timekeeper of Goa’s changing face,” I later tell Libia. She laughs. It’s an infectious laugh that coaxes me to imagine her younger self, then the secretary of the Goa Youth League, parading across the streets of Mumbai, partaking in morchas (protests), and speaking truth to power, untethered by fear. Even as India gained independence in 1947, Goa remained under the grip of Portuguese dictator António de Oliveira Salazar, who arrested anyone who dared to oppose the rules. Libia explains, “You could not say ‘Jai Hind’. There was censorship. Even an invitation card had to be censored because they were suspicious. The vigilance of the Portuguese was such that if four people were talking on the road, there would be police trying to find out whether they were fermenting something.” But juxtaposed against these harsh memories are also fond ones. Libia shares, “We’d spend most of our vacations in Goa. We would arrive by ship; there were hundreds of us, and we’d come with all our provisions.” To Libia and the other young people, Goa was home — and all they wanted was to see it free. Driven by that longing, the youth did everything they could to spur action within the liberation movement. In 1954, they planned a satyagraha; people flocked to the borders of Goa demanding freedom from Portuguese rule. But they were fired upon. Following this, a blockade was imposed on the borders. “No one could come into the state, and no one could leave. Goa was completely cut off from India. The boycott led to starvation within the state, but the Portuguese brought in food from Karachi,” Libia explains. The shelves of Libia Lobo Sardesai and Vaman Sardesai’s home also hold gallantry medals awarded by the Indian Army and Navy. Then, in 1955, the people of Bombay once again decided to oppose the dictatorship. “We decided to go in larger numbers. Around 3,000 people registered themselves for the chain satyagraha, but this group was shot down as well,” she shares. The patriots were stumped. Hope arrived in the form of Vaman Sardesai, who’d spent nine months in jail for propagating anti-colonial literature and was stripped of his civic and political rights for five years. Arrested during his second year of medical school, he found himself unable to continue his studies or rebuild his life easily in Goa. Eventually, he went to Sevagram Ashram in Maharashtra, where he stayed for four years. Later, he was given a job at All India Radio by the Government of India, where Libia also worked. He was tasked with recording the views of Goans in Bombay, which were then sent to the External Services Division in Delhi and broadcast to the world so that an international audience could learn about what was happening in Goa. An idea soon began to take shape among the patriots: what if radio — the very medium through which information was controlled — could instead be used to reach the people of Goa and reconnect them with the rest of India? From that vision was born the ‘Voice of Freedom’ radio station. Its first broadcast was scheduled for 25 November 1955, a date deliberately chosen because it marked exactly 445 years since the Portuguese conquest of Goa. ‘We shed our blood for India’ As Libia and I speak, our conversation meanders through many things: Goa, her story, my story, the music playing outside in the public square, and of course, her love story with Vaman. She begins by describing their bond as a result of their shared passion for their homeland. While the two married much later in 1964, they were each other's companions and confidantes for years before that. Much of their bond was shaped by the six years they spent together, from 1955 to 1961, in forested areas, broadcasting through their radio station incognito and undercover so they wouldn’t be detected. Was it an easy ‘yes’? Libia laughs, “I told my family I had been transferred for six months to Belgaum.” The couple was initially joined by another patriot, Nicolau Menezes, while they set up base on the Maharashtra border, close to Sawantwadi. However, in a bid to move somewhere more discreet, they shifted to Castlerock Hill, closer to Karnataka. At a height of 2,000 metres, in the midst of three tin sheets (which the team used for installation and habitation), they broadcast. The electricity for the transmission was obtained from the Western India Portuguese Railway Station. The years that followed were filled with insurmountable odds. “There were reptiles and leeches everywhere,” Libia recalls. “We used to wear gumboots, but the leeches would still crawl inside them. Our blood would drip constantly; we shed so much blood. Though we were never shot by a gun, we shed so much blood over those six years.” Together, Libia Lobo Sardesai and Vaman Sardesai dedicated their lives to Goa’s liberation movement — a contribution later recognised with the Padma Shri, awarded to Vaman in 1992 and Libia in 2025. One day, on 16 December 1961, Libia and Vaman were driven in a military jeep to Belgaum, where they were asked to broadcast from a new location. The next day, at one-hour intervals, the couple broadcast a direct message from India's defence minister addressed to the Portuguese governor general, asking him to surrender. They were met with silence. Operation Vijay was given the go-ahead. Naval warships, the Indian Air Force, and the Indian Army joined forces to launch a full-scale assault on Portuguese strongholds across Goa. The Portuguese forces finally surrendered. When Libia and Vaman were given this news by the Chief of Army Staff, General J M Chaudhary, Libia recalls taking a flower from the garden and giving it to him. He asked her, ‘Now, Kumari Lobo, what next? What do you want to do?’ Libia replied, “There are no people left to listen to me because the radio depends on waves. I must go to the skies and tell them.” “The next morning, we got a call from the Army asking us to come at 6:30 am. They said, ‘You wanted to go to the skies and tell people, didn’t you? So we are going to send you by plane.’ But they warned us that the plane was at risk of being shot down. We said, ‘That’s alright. At least we will fall on free Goa.’.” And so, a plan flew across Goa carrying the brave duo who’d weathered innumerable challenges to ensure the people of Goa were not cut off from India. Lobo and Vaman married exactly three years later, on Liberation Day. Vaman became Goa’s first director of panchayats, and later an IAS officer, the co-convenor of the Indian National Trust for Art and Cultural Heritage (INTACH), and the ambassador of India to Angola. Libia rose to the ranks of Goa’s first director of tourism, and then later gave up the job to start her law practice, blazing a trail as one of the state’s first women lawyers. As we come to the end of our chat, Libia tells me about coming across Portuguese army commandant Major Filipe de Barros Rodrigues's report about the Voice of Freedom. The report states that the Voice of Freedom had “assumed the command of the entire propaganda and has maintained its aggressiveness and militancy” and that the station “threatens, criticises, persuades, explains, changes colours, alters perspectives, but in everything it says, it carries a sharp stiletto”, and was “the only voice which was hurting us (Portuguese) at close range”. As Libia finishes reading, I also realise that I never asked any questions. Just the one at the start: “So, tell me how it all began.”
